g.data-narration: Behavioral modeling in Verilog HDL is a high-level abstraction technique that describes how a circuit behaves over time in response to inputs.
g.text: High-Level Abstraction for Digital Circuit Design

g.data-narration: Unlike structural modeling using gates or dataflow using continuous assignments, behavioral modeling describes WHAT the circuit does, not HOW it's built.
Behavioral modeling is essential for designing sequential circuits, state machines, and complex control logic with algorithmic descriptions.
The always block is the fundamental construct in behavioral modeling. It continuously monitors its sensitivity list and executes statements when triggered.
The sensitivity list specifies which signals trigger the always block. Combinational needs all inputs; sequential only needs clock edge.
g.text: always @(a or b or sel) // ALL inputs

g.data-narration: If-else statements provide decision-making capability in behavioral modeling, executing different code paths based on condition evaluation.
The case statement compares an expression against multiple values, executing the matching branch - essential for multiplexers and state machines.
This is a critical distinction: blocking assignments (=) execute sequentially, while non-blocking assignments (<=) schedule simultaneously but update at time step end.
g.text: y = x | c; // Uses NEW x value
q2 <= q1; // Uses OLD q1 value

g.data-narration: For blocking assignments, each statement completes before the next begins - like writing on paper where each step depends on the previous.
Non-blocking assignments schedule simultaneously and update at the end of the time step - both statements see old values, preventing race conditions.
g.text: Timing: Non-Blocking Assignments Execute in Parallel
Both scheduled together, update at end of time step

g.data-narration: Initial blocks execute once at simulation time zero - essential for testbenches but NOT synthesizable for actual hardware design.
g.text: Initial blocks cannot be synthesized to hardware. Use only in testbenches.

g.data-narration: This 4:1 multiplexer example shows behavioral modeling: declare module inputs as reg for output, use always block with case statement.
g.text: module mux4to1(input a,b,c,d, input[1:0] sel, output reg y);
always @(a or b or c or d or sel) begin

g.data-narration: This D flip-flop demonstrates sequential logic: use non-blocking assignment, include reset in sensitivity list for asynchronous operation.
g.text: Example 2: D Flip-Flop with Async Reset
module dff_async(input clk, rst_n, d, output reg q);
always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin

g.data-narration: This 4-bit counter with load and clear shows hierarchical priority: check async clear first, then load, then increment.
g.text: module counter(input clk, clr, load, input[3:0] data, output reg[3:0] count);
always @(posedge clk or posedge clr) begin

g.data-narration: Remember these essential rules: combinational uses blocking, sequential uses non-blocking, always include all inputs in sensitivity lists.
g.text: Combinational: always @(all_inputs) | Sequential: always @(posedge clk) | Block: = for comb, <= for seq

g.data-narration: Avoid these mistakes: using blocking in sequential blocks causes race conditions, incomplete sensitivity lists, forgetting default cases.
